does: This paper reframes mechanistic interpretability research through the lens of cognitive science history, arguing that current MI methods represent a shift analogous to the cognitive revolution that transformed psychology in the mid-20th century. The work distinguishes between different interpretability objectives—explaining behavior versus understanding internal representations and algorithms—and uses cognitive science frameworks to clarify what mechanistic interpretability actually aims to achieve. By drawing parallels between the challenges of interpreting neural networks and decades of cognitive science research on the human brain, the paper identifies how MI can move beyond treating models as black boxes by studying learned features, implicit algorithms, and representational structures within foundation models like large language models.
connects: This work builds on two converging research traditions: mechanistic interpretability research that has emerged from deep learning (studying attention heads, circuit-based explanations, and learned features) and classical cognitive science approaches to understanding complex intelligent systems including neuroscience, cognitive psychology, and philosophy of mind. It responds to the foundational tension in interpretability between behavioral explanation methods (like saliency maps and SHAP values) and approaches seeking to reverse-engineer internal mechanisms. The paper engages with the historical "cognitive revolution" that shifted psychology from behaviorism to cognitivism by emphasizing internal mental representations, drawing lessons from how cognitive science resolved analogous questions about explaining intelligent behavior without direct access to internal processes.
enables: By grounding MI in cognitive science, this work enables more rigorous framing of interpretability research priorities and methodologies, potentially accelerating progress by importing decades of cognitive science insights into deep learning. It opens pathways for mechanistic interpretability to move toward genuinely explanatory theories of how neural networks compute rather than mere correlational behavioral explanations. This perspective could guide the design of more targeted experiments, suggest new interpretability techniques adapted from neuroscience methods, and establish clearer connections between understanding artificial and biological intelligence. The framework may also help identify which interpretability questions are tractable versus fundamentally difficult, and clarify what successful mechanistic understanding would actually require, positioning future work to build more reliable and transparent AI systems.
